text: The surgeon observes that the hernia is adhered to the omentum and the anterior abdominal wall, with visible adhesions present at the hernia ring.
An assistant aids in reducing the irreducible hernia, working to free it from adhesions with the omentum and anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon cuts a portion of the medial umbilical ligament on the left side, creating additional access to the omentum adhered in the area.
The surgeon fixes the Vipro II mesh in place and sutures it to the peritoneum along the inferior edge, completing the mesh placement in the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon places the Vipro II mesh directly over the hernia site and secures it using sutures and a tacker.
An assistant pushes the omentum from above, gradually repositioning all irreducible omental content back into the abdominal cavity.
The surgical field shows no further adhesiolysis being performed in the area, indicating that the surgeon has completed this step of the procedure.
The surgeon completes the separation of the omentum from the abdominal cavity, ensuring the omentum is fully repositioned within the abdomen.
The surgeon pushes the omentum towards the upper abdomen, revealing the head of the hernia defect.
The surgeon introduces the telescope to visualize the left side indirect complete inguinal hernia, revealing the hernia sac protruding through the inguinal canal.

The surgeon introduces a Vipro-II mesh into the surgical field, spreading it over the anterior abdominal wall, covering areas including the triangle of doom, triangle of pain, and trapezoid of disaster.

The surgeon secures the outer edge of the Vipro-II mesh using absorbable tackers, positioned laterally to avoid capturing the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve (LFCN) or genitofemoral nerve (GFN), minimizing postoperative pain. The mesh is anchored over the arc of the transversus abdominis muscle, with plans to proceed to the inner crowning.
The Y-Pro-II mesh is visible, showing its construction with internal vicryl fibers and external proline fibers.
The surgeon identifies the Cooper ligament during the hernia repair procedure, ensuring no adhesions are formed in the surrounding area.
The surgeon secures the inferior medial end of the Vipro-II mesh over the Cooper ligament using an absorbent tacker, which is felt as a hard structure by the tip of the tacker.

The surgeon positions the mesh over the anterior abdominal wall, targeting the rectus abdominis muscle for fixation. Care is taken to avoid firing the tacker directly into the bladder, ensuring placement medial or lateral to the medial umbilical ligament.

The surgeon uses the eye pump technique for inguinal hernia repair, firing absorbable tackers above the inguinal ligament and medially over the medial umbilical ligament onto the rectus abdominis muscle.
The surgeon avoids firing the mesh inferiorly due to the presence of anatomical landmarks known as the trapezoid of disaster, triangle of doom, and triangle of pain.
The surgeon demonstrates the outer crowning of the mesh, positioning it over the anterior abdominal wall during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon performs the inner crowning over and above the inguinal ligament, focusing on the precise placement of the mesh in relation to the anatomical landmarks.
The surgeon uses tackers to secure the inner crowning of the mesh, ensuring it covers approximately six centimeters from the healthy margin of the hernia defect.

The surgeon carefully places a suture at the inferior margin, ensuring it only holds the peritoneum without involving nearby vessels or the ureter. A Dundee jamming knot with a pre-tied loop is used, which locks automatically upon pulling.
The surgeon begins a starter knot to secure the inferior edge of the mesh to the underlying tissue, ensuring the mesh is properly anchored.

The surgeon continues suturing at one-centimeter intervals, carefully pulling the peritoneum superficially and taking bites with at least a one-centimeter margin, avoiding deeper penetration to protect underlying structures.

The surgeon locks each suture, ensuring the mesh is securely moughing the margin, similar to tailoring cloth edges. This technique prevents any space through which the omentum or bowel could incarcerate, promoting fibrosis and adhesion of the mesh over time.

The surgeon carefully passes the needle parallel to the peritoneum, taking bites and locking the suture while avoiding the nearby iliac vein, internal iliac artery, external iliac vein, and external iliac artery.
The surgeon pulls the peritoneum towards themselves to distance it from the vessels, allowing for the safe muffing of the inferior surface of the mesh, ensuring it covers the entire vessel.
The surgeon ensures that the mesh is positioned such that no structures are visible anterior to it, indicating complete coverage and secure placement over the surgical site.
The external iliac artery is visible, and the surgeon pulls the peritoneum. On the left side, the sigmoid colon may require additional manipulation if it is not positioned cooperatively.
The surgeon mobilizes the sigmoid colon, ensuring that the internal needle passes through the suture at least one centimeter deep to secure the peritoneum.
The surgeon uses sutures to secure the peritoneum, ensuring it is both tucked and traversed by the suture. The process of muffing continues, with each step being locked securely.
The surgeon takes a deep bite in a safe area without muscle, using a loop within loop technique to secure the suture and extract the needle.
The surgeon demonstrates the loop within loop technique by pulling the non-needle end to lock the suture in place, securing the mesh during hernia repair.
The surgeon demonstrates the technique of creating a double lock by pulling the non-needle end of the suture, ensuring the needle remains away from the loop within loop configuration.

The surgeon passes the needle through the loop to perform an abutting termination, similar to techniques used in open surgery to close the peritoneum or rectus. The excess suture is cut, and the needle is removed from the surgical field.
